questions.question: Your team uses a Lakehouse (queried through its SQL analytics endpoint) and a separate Warehouse in the same workspace. Which TWO statements about modifying data through T-SQL are correct? (Select TWO.)

options.A: Both endpoints accept the same DML because they share one engine
options.B: An UPDATE against a Warehouse table is supported
options.C: Correcting a Lakehouse table value requires Spark, a dataflow, or a pipeline
options.D: An UPDATE against a table in the Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint is supported

questions.explanation: The Warehouse is a fully transactional T-SQL engine, so an UPDATE against a Warehouse table is supported. The Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint, by contrast, is read-only over the underlying Delta tables, so correcting a Lakehouse table value must go through Spark, a dataflow, or a pipeline. The two claims that the endpoints share one engine and accept the same DML, and that an UPDATE against the Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint is supported, are the traps: the endpoints look alike, but only the Warehouse is read/write, so they do not share an identical writable surface.

questions.question: A data engineer is writing the first ingestion routine for a new Fabric Warehouse and wants the loader Microsoft recommends for high-throughput bulk loads from an Azure storage account. Which T-SQL statement should they standardize on?

options.B: INSERT ... SELECT FROM OPENROWSET
options.C: SELECT ... INTO from an external path
options.D: BULK INSERT

questions.explanation: COPY INTO is the recommended high-throughput ingestion statement for Fabric Warehouse, with parallel loading, rejected-row handling, and flexible authentication. BULK INSERT is genuinely supported in Fabric Warehouse, but it exists mainly to let you reuse SQL Server / Azure SQL code; it is not the recommended path for new ingestion, which makes it the strongest distractor.

questions.question: Regulators require that support staff querying a customer table never see full credit-card numbers, yet the stored values must stay intact so the billing service can still read them. Which Warehouse capability fits?

options.B: Dynamic data masking
options.C: OneLake data-access roles
options.D: Column-level security

questions.explanation: Dynamic data masking rewrites the value at query time for principals without UNMASK, while the stored data is unchanged and a privileged service can still read it. Column-level security is the near-miss: it would block the column entirely rather than show a masked form, and it gives no way for the billing service to read a masked-but-present value through the same column grant.

questions.question: A dimension table must be (re)built every morning from cleansed staging data in one set-based, fully parallelized statement that also defines the new table's schema from the query. Which statement does this in a Fabric Warehouse?

options.B: INSERT INTO ... SELECT into a pre-created table
options.C: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT
options.D: SELECT ... INTO with OPTION (FOR TIMESTAMP AS OF)

questions.explanation: CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS) creates the table (inferring its schema from the query) and populates it from the SELECT in a single optimized operation, ideal for rebuilding a dimension. INSERT INTO ... SELECT into a pre-created table is the trap because it is also set-based and fast, but it requires the target table to already exist with a defined schema, so it does not satisfy the 'define schema from the query' requirement.

questions.question: In a Fabric Warehouse, a user is a member of a role that has been granted SELECT on a table, but the same user has a permission set directly that should stop them reading it. Which T-SQL permission produces an unconditional block that the role's grant cannot override?

options.B: GRANT SELECT WITH GRANT OPTION removed
options.C: REVOKE SELECT
options.D: DENY SELECT

questions.explanation: DENY SELECT explicitly prohibits the action and wins over any GRANT, whether direct or inherited; precedence is DENY > GRANT. REVOKE SELECT is the near-miss: it only removes a previously granted (or denied) permission, so a role-level GRANT would still let the user in. Dropping the role membership with ALTER ROLE ... DROP MEMBER changes the user's group, not an unconditional object block.

questions.question: A pipeline lands messy daily extracts that need deduplication and type-casting before they reach the production star schema. The lead asks why the design routes everything through an intermediate table first. Which TWO benefits best justify a staging layer? (Select TWO.)

options.A: It lets raw data be validated and reshaped before it is published to dimensions and facts
options.B: It enforces row-level security on the incoming records automatically
options.C: It guarantees referential integrity through enforced foreign keys
options.D: It compresses the data to Parquet before it is written to OneLake
options.E: It decouples ingestion timing from transformation, so a slow load doesn't block reporting tables

questions.explanation: A staging layer provides a place to validate, cleanse, deduplicate, and reshape raw data before it is published to dimensions and facts, and it decouples ingestion timing from transformation so a slow load doesn't block reporting tables. The distractors do not hold: row-level security is a query-time access control, not a load-time property of staging; Fabric Warehouse foreign keys are not enforced, so a staging table cannot guarantee referential integrity; and OneLake already stores Warehouse data as Parquet regardless of staging.

questions.question: An engineer migrating from a Synapse dedicated SQL pool needs an accurate mental model of how a large Fabric Warehouse fact table is physically managed. Which TWO statements are correct? (Select TWO.)

options.A: You choose a hash, round-robin, or replicated distribution per table
options.B: Data distribution is managed automatically; there is no distribution clause
options.C: You do not create traditional indexes; the engine optimizes the Delta-Parquet layout
options.D: A clustered columnstore index must be defined before the table is queryable

questions.explanation: Fabric Warehouse abstracts physical storage: data distribution is managed automatically with no distribution clause, and you do not create traditional indexes because the engine optimizes the Delta-Parquet layout for you. Choosing a hash/round-robin/replicated distribution per table and defining a clustered columnstore index before querying are the Synapse-habit traps, where those exact knobs existed and mattered but have no equivalent in Fabric Warehouse.

questions.question: Analysts must keep seeing every order row, but two compensation columns have to be completely inaccessible to them, returning an error if referenced. Salary figures should never be merely blurred. Which approach meets the requirement most precisely?

options.A: Issue DENY SELECT on the two columns to the analyst role
options.B: Apply dynamic data masking with the default() mask to the two columns
options.C: Move the two columns to a separate table and revoke SELECT on the base table
options.D: Bind a security policy with a filter predicate on the analyst role

questions.explanation: Column-level security via DENY SELECT on the two specific columns blocks access entirely (a query naming them errors) while all rows stay visible, which matches 'inaccessible, not blurred.' Dynamic data masking with default() only obscures the displayed value and still lets the query run, so it fails the 'never merely blurred' constraint. Binding a security policy with a filter predicate is row-level security, which filters rows rather than columns, and moving the columns to a separate table is a heavier redesign that also hides them from the base table for everyone.

questions.question: Within one workspace, a report query must join a conformed dimension that lives in a Warehouse to a fact table exposed through a Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint, in a single T-SQL statement, with no data movement. What enables this?

options.A: Three-part-name cross-item queries, since both share the workspace SQL engine surface
options.B: A linked server registered between the two items
options.C: A Spark notebook, because T-SQL cannot reach across two items
options.D: A shortcut from the Warehouse to the Lakehouse table, then a local join
options.E: An external table in the Warehouse pointing at the Lakehouse files

questions.explanation: Fabric supports cross-database (cross-item) T-SQL within a workspace, so a three-part-name query joins the Warehouse and Lakehouse-endpoint tables without copying any data. A shortcut from the Warehouse to the Lakehouse table is a real Fabric feature and is tempting, but shortcuts are created in a Lakehouse over storage locations, not from a Warehouse over another item's SQL table, so it does not deliver this join. Linked servers do not exist in Fabric, a Spark notebook is unnecessary because T-SQL can reach across items, and an external table pointing at the Lakehouse files would move away from the no-movement cross-item query.

questions.question: You must load 200 GB of daily Parquet files into a fact table at maximum throughput, and any malformed rows must be diverted so the load still completes. While configuring COPY INTO, which detail is the critical gotcha to verify?

options.A: Use CTAS over the Parquet path with TRY_CAST on every column
options.B: The ERRORFILE / rejected-row redirect applies only to CSV, so Parquet input cannot divert bad rows that way
options.C: Replace COPY INTO with row-by-row INSERTs to capture each failure
options.D: Set FILE_TYPE = 'CSV' so the rejected-row option becomes available
options.E: Wrap the COPY INTO in an explicit BEGIN TRAN so failures roll back

questions.explanation: COPY INTO with FILE_TYPE = 'PARQUET' gives the throughput, but the ERRORFILE / rejected-row redirect is documented as applying to CSV only, so the team cannot rely on it to quarantine bad Parquet rows. Setting FILE_TYPE = 'CSV' just to unlock error handling is the trap: it would discard the high-throughput Parquet format and misread the source. Switching to CTAS with TRY_CAST, replacing COPY INTO with row-by-row INSERTs, or wrapping the load in BEGIN TRAN all change the throughput-oriented approach; the correct planning answer is to recognize this Parquet limitation.

questions.question: Compliance dictates that each nurse sees only patients in their own department while administrators see all rows, and all records must remain in one shared table. Which TWO building blocks together implement this in a Fabric Warehouse? (Select TWO.)

options.A: A CREATE SECURITY POLICY that binds the function as a filter predicate on the table
options.B: An inline table-valued function that returns rows matching the caller's department
options.C: A dynamic data masking rule on the department column
options.D: A DENY SELECT on the department column for the nurse role

questions.explanation: Row-level security is implemented by an inline table-valued function that evaluates the caller's identity and returns only matching rows, plus a CREATE SECURITY POLICY that binds that function as a FILTER predicate on the table, keeping all data in one shared table. A dynamic data masking rule and a DENY SELECT on the department column both act on columns, not rows, so neither restricts which patients are returned.

questions.question: A surrogate-key design is proposed for a large sales fact joining to several dimensions. A reviewer wants the choices that genuinely improve join performance and storage in a Fabric Warehouse. Which TWO statements are correct? (Select TWO.)

options.A: Narrow integer surrogate keys keep joins efficient and storage compact
options.B: GUID natural keys widen rows and degrade join performance versus integers
options.C: Collapsing everything into one wide denormalized table is required for star-schema joins
options.D: Declaring (unenforced) foreign keys lets the optimizer use them as join accelerators
options.E: Enforced foreign key constraints in the Warehouse physically index the join columns

questions.explanation: Narrow integer surrogate keys produce compact rows and fast joins, and GUID natural keys are wider and degrade join performance versus integers. The foreign-key claims are traps: Fabric Warehouse foreign keys are metadata only and not enforced, so neither declaring unenforced foreign keys as 'join accelerators' nor expecting enforced constraints to physically index the join columns is correct. Collapsing everything into one wide denormalized table contradicts star-schema modeling, which keeps dimensions separate.

questions.question: User 'Bob' belongs to the 'Analysts' role, which holds GRANT SELECT on dbo.Orders. A separate statement issued DENY SELECT on dbo.Orders to Bob directly. What is the effective result when Bob queries dbo.Orders?

options.A: He receives a permissions-conflict error at connect time
options.B: He can read only columns that carry no mask
options.C: He can read it, because a role grant outranks a user-level deny
options.D: He is blocked, because the explicit deny overrides the inherited grant

questions.explanation: DENY always takes precedence over GRANT regardless of whether the grant was direct or inherited via a role, so Bob is blocked because the explicit deny overrides the inherited grant. The claim that a role grant outranks a user-level deny inverts the precedence rule and is the most common misconception this question targets; the remaining choices invent behaviors (a connect-time conflict error, or mask-based column filtering) that SQL permission evaluation does not exhibit here.

questions.question: Each night a dimension must absorb a feed: rows whose business key already exists get their attributes overwritten, and brand-new keys are added. No history is kept. Which single statement implements this most directly?

options.A: MERGE on the business key with WHEN MATCHED UPDATE and WHEN NOT MATCHED INSERT
options.B: INSERT ... SELECT with WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE handling
options.C: TRUNCATE then COPY INTO the full feed
options.D: UPDATE ... FROM the feed, then a separate INSERT for new keys

questions.explanation: A MERGE keyed on the business key performs the SCD Type 1 upsert in one statement: matched rows are updated and unmatched rows inserted. The separate UPDATE ... FROM followed by an INSERT for new keys is the closest near-miss; it produces the same data but is two statements with a race window between them, so it is not the 'single statement, most direct' answer. WHEN NOT MATCHED BY SOURCE is a MERGE clause and is not valid on a plain INSERT, and TRUNCATE-then-COPY INTO reloads everything rather than performing a keyed upsert.

questions.question: A Power BI Direct Lake model must combine cleansed facts kept in a Lakehouse with conformed dimensions maintained in a Warehouse, with the least storage duplication. Which design works?

options.A: Copy the Warehouse dimensions into the Lakehouse as Delta tables, then point Direct Lake at the Lakehouse
options.B: Reference the tables across both items in one semantic model, since each persists Delta-Parquet in OneLake
options.C: Switch the report to Import mode with an hourly refresh
options.D: Mirror the Warehouse into the Lakehouse and build Direct Lake on the mirror

questions.explanation: Both Warehouse and Lakehouse store data as Delta-Parquet in OneLake, so a single Direct Lake semantic model can reference tables from both items without copying anything, giving the least storage duplication. Copying the Warehouse dimensions into the Lakehouse is the tempting duplication trap (it works but copies the data). Mirroring targets external operational databases, not a Warehouse-to-Lakehouse copy, and would also duplicate storage, and switching to Import mode abandons Direct Lake and duplicates data into the model.

questions.question: After an email() mask was added to a column, the nightly ETL service principal began reading masked values and its merge keyed on email now fails. You must restore the ETL while keeping analysts masked, using the least-privilege change. What do you do?

options.A: Grant UNMASK to the service principal
options.B: Drop the mask from the column
options.C: Replace masking with column-level security
options.D: Grant CONTROL on the table to the service principal
options.E: Add the service principal to the workspace Admin role

questions.explanation: Granting UNMASK to the service principal is the targeted permission that lets it see real values for the keyed merge while everyone else stays masked. Granting CONTROL on the table or adding the principal to the workspace Admin role would also reveal the data but hand out far more than needed, violating least privilege. Dropping the mask exposes the data to everyone, defeating the control, and replacing masking with column-level security would block the column rather than reveal it to the ETL.

questions.question:
A Fabric Warehouse table Sales(Region NVARCHAR, Amount INT) is queried as follows.

```sql
SELECT Region, SUM(Amount) AS Total
FROM Sales
GROUP BY Region
HAVING SUM(Amount) > 1000
ORDER BY Total DESC;
```

What does it return?

options.A: Every individual row where Amount exceeds 1000, ordered by Amount
options.B: All regions with their totals, ignoring the 1000 cutoff
options.C: An error, because HAVING cannot reference an aggregate
options.D: One row per Region whose total Amount exceeds 1000, highest total first

questions.explanation: GROUP BY aggregates per Region, HAVING SUM(Amount) > 1000 filters to the groups whose total exceeds 1000 (HAVING is exactly for post-aggregate filtering), and ORDER BY Total DESC sorts descending, so the result is one row per Region whose total Amount exceeds 1000, highest total first. The 'every individual row where Amount exceeds 1000' choice is the trap that confuses WHERE-style row filtering with HAVING's group filtering; the 'error because HAVING cannot reference an aggregate' choice invents a restriction T-SQL does not impose, since HAVING is built for aggregates; and 'all regions ignoring the cutoff' ignores the HAVING clause entirely.

questions.question:
Employees has 10 rows; BonusAmount is NULL in 3 of them and DeptId is NULL in 1. What does this return in a Fabric Warehouse?

```sql
SELECT
  COUNT(*)            AS C1,
  COUNT(BonusAmount)  AS C2,
  COUNT(DISTINCT DeptId) AS C3
FROM Employees;
```

DeptId has values: 5 rows = 1, 4 rows = 2, 1 row = NULL.
questions.explanation: COUNT(*) counts all rows = 10. COUNT(BonusAmount) ignores the 3 NULLs = 7. COUNT(DISTINCT DeptId) ignores the NULL and counts the distinct non-NULL values {1, 2} = 2, giving C1 = 10, C2 = 7, C3 = 2. The result that reports C3 = 3 is the trap: it wrongly treats NULL as a third distinct DeptId value, but COUNT(DISTINCT col) excludes NULL. The result with C2 = 10 wrongly counts the NULL bonuses, and the one with C1 = 7 wrongly drops rows from COUNT(*).

questions.question: You want to create and populate an aggregate table from staging in one statement in a Fabric Warehouse. Which statement uses CREATE TABLE AS SELECT (CTAS) syntax correctly?

options.A: SELECT Region, SUM(Amount) AS Total INTO dbo.SalesAgg FROM dbo.Staging GROUP BY Region;
options.B: MERGE INTO dbo.SalesAgg USING dbo.Staging AS s ON 1 = 0 WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT (Region, Total) VALUES (s.Region, s.Amount);
options.C: INSERT INTO dbo.SalesAgg AS SELECT Region, SUM(Amount) FROM dbo.Staging GROUP BY Region;
options.D: CREATE TABLE dbo.SalesAgg AS SELECT Region, SUM(Amount) AS Total FROM dbo.Staging GROUP BY Region;
options.E: CREATE TABLE dbo.SalesAgg (Region NVARCHAR(50), Total INT) AS SELECT Region, SUM(Amount) FROM dbo.Staging GROUP BY Region;

questions.explanation: Fabric Warehouse CTAS uses the form CREATE TABLE <name> AS SELECT ...;, which both defines and loads the table in one operation, so the statement that reads CREATE TABLE dbo.SalesAgg AS SELECT ... GROUP BY Region; is the canonical, correct CTAS. The SELECT ... INTO form also creates-and-loads but is not CTAS syntax, so it does not answer 'which uses CTAS correctly.' The MERGE requires the target table to already exist; the INSERT INTO ... AS SELECT form is invalid syntax; and supplying an explicit column list together with AS SELECT is also invalid CTAS syntax.

questions.question:
A column is masked as shown, and an unprivileged user selects the row whose stored value is '5551234567'.

```sql
ALTER TABLE Customers
  ALTER COLUMN Phone
  ADD MASKED WITH (FUNCTION = 'partial(0,"XXX-XXX-",4)');
```

What value does that user see?

options.B: 555XXX-XXX-
options.C: XXX-XXX-4567
options.D: XXX-XXX-5551

questions.explanation: partial(prefix, padding, suffix) exposes the first 'prefix' characters (here 0), substitutes the literal padding 'XXX-XXX-', and exposes the last 'suffix' characters (here 4). For '5551234567' that yields 'XXX-XXX-4567'. The '555XXX-XXX-' option is the trap that exposes the first characters instead of the last four, reversing the prefix/suffix roles of the partial mask; '5551234567' would mean no masking at all; and 'XXX-XXX-5551' takes the wrong four characters for the suffix.
